name: unit-files
context: Understand unit file locations, precedence, drop-ins, templates, and override rules before editing systemd
info: This context node explains how systemd finds and merges unit files. It covers the priority-ordered search paths for system and user managers, how `[Unit]`, type-specific sections such as `[Service]`, and `[Install]` fit together, how `.d` drop-ins override existing units without editing vendor files, how `.wants/` and `.requires/` create dependencies, and how templates and `systemd-escape` help when a single unit definition needs multiple instances. Agents need this knowledge whenever a unit seems to load the wrong file, an override is not taking effect, or a new unit must be placed in the right directory for the intended manager scope.
triggers: where should i put a systemd unit file
why is systemd loading the wrong file
create a drop-in override for a service
body:
# Unit Files

Unit files are INI-style files loaded from a priority-ordered search path. For system units, `/etc/systemd/system` overrides `/run/systemd/system`, which in turn overrides files installed by the distribution under `/usr/lib/systemd/system`. For user units, `~/.config/systemd/user` has the highest everyday precedence.

Use the actual manager state, not guesses, to confirm what is loaded:

```bash
systemd-analyze unit-paths
systemctl show my-app.service -p FragmentPath -p UnitFileState --value
```

Use drop-ins when editing packaged units instead of modifying vendor files in place. A directory such as `/etc/systemd/system/my-app.service.d/` can add or replace directives after the main file is parsed. Templates such as `worker@.service` create instances like `worker@alpha.service`, and `%i` expands to the instance name. If you need to turn paths into legal unit identifiers, use `systemd-escape --path`.

This node points to [[systemd/services]] and [[systemd/timers]] for authoring, and to [[systemd/troubleshooting#overrides-and-stale-config]] when overrides do not behave as expected.

sections.title: Load path
sections.body: System managers load units from a precedence-ordered path that starts with `/etc/systemd/system`, then runtime and generator paths, then `/usr/local/lib/systemd/system` and `/usr/lib/systemd/system`. User managers use `~/.config/systemd/user`, `/etc/systemd/user`, runtime paths, and installed user-unit directories. Earlier paths override later ones. `systemd-analyze unit-paths` shows compiled paths; `systemctl show -p UnitPath --value` shows what the running manager actually uses.

sections.title: Structure and dependencies
sections.body: Most units use `[Unit]` for description and dependencies, a type-specific section such as `[Service]` or `[Timer]`, and `[Install]` for enablement targets and aliases. Dependency directories such as `foo.service.wants/` and `foo.service.requires/` are another way to attach units without editing the unit file directly. `systemctl enable` creates install symlinks from `[Install]` metadata.

sections.title: Overrides and drop-ins
sections.body: Drop-ins live in `NAME.service.d/*.conf` and are merged after the main unit file. They are the correct place for local changes to packaged services. Higher-precedence drop-ins in `/etc` beat drop-ins in `/run`, which beat drop-ins in `/usr/lib`. Use drop-ins instead of editing vendor files directly so updates do not erase your changes.

sections.title: Templates and escaping
sections.body: A template unit ends with `@.service` and gains an instance string between `@` and the suffix when activated, for example `worker@alpha.service`. Inside the file, specifiers such as `%i` expose the instance name. Use `systemd-escape` when arbitrary strings or filesystem paths must become safe unit names. Masking a unit is implemented by an empty file or a symlink to `/dev/null` so the manager cannot load or activate it.
